title: Avoid using eval() on untrusted input
rationale: Using eval() to execute arbitrary strings as Python code is a well-documented anti-pattern because it can execute malicious code if any part of the input string originates from an untrusted source, and it also makes the program's behavior difficult to statically analyze.
good_example:
import ast
user_input = input("Enter expression: ")
result = ast.literal_eval(user_input)

title: Avoid catching the base Exception class broadly
rationale: Catching the generic Exception class is considered an anti-pattern because it hides the specific type of failure, makes it impossible to handle different error conditions differently, and can mask programming errors as if they were expected runtime conditions.

title: Avoid string concatenation in a loop
rationale: Repeatedly concatenating strings inside a loop using the + operator is an anti-pattern because each concatenation creates a new string object, resulting in quadratic time complexity for what should be a linear operation; using str.join or a list buffer is the recommended alternative.

title: Avoid comparing types with == instead of isinstance
rationale: Comparing an object's type directly using '== type(x)' is an anti-pattern because it breaks polymorphism and fails for subclasses, whereas isinstance() correctly accounts for inheritance hierarchies.

title: Avoid unused variables
rationale: Assigning a value to a variable that is never subsequently used is an anti-pattern because it indicates either dead code, an incomplete refactor, or a logic error where the wrong variable was referenced, and it adds noise that obscures the program's real behavior.
bad_example:
def compute(a, b):
    total = a + b
    result = a * b
    return total

title: Avoid deeply nested loops and conditionals
rationale: Deeply nested loops and conditional blocks are an anti-pattern because each additional level of nesting increases cyclomatic complexity and makes the code substantially harder to read, test, and reason about; extracting nested logic into separate functions is the recommended fix.

title: Avoid unexplained magic numbers
rationale: Embedding unexplained numeric literals directly in logic is an anti-pattern because the meaning and origin of the value is lost to future readers; assigning the value to a named constant documents its purpose and makes future changes a single-point edit.

title: Avoid inconsistent return types from a single function
rationale: A function that returns a value in one branch but implicitly returns None in another is an anti-pattern because callers cannot predict the return type without reading the full function body, increasing the risk of AttributeError or TypeError downstream.
